name: Vinitaly & Vinitaly and the City
tagline: The world's biggest Italian wine fair spills out of the fairgrounds and into Verona's Renaissance piazzas for a weekend of open-air tastings.
dates: Vinitaly (trade fair): 11–14 April 2027, Veronafiere; Vinitaly and the City (public festival): c. 9–11 April 2027 (expected, the Fri–Sun straddling the fair's opening)
history: Vinitaly began in 1967 as the 'Giornate del vino italiano' (Italian Wine Days), a modest gathering at the Verona fairgrounds meant to give Italy's fragmented wine industry a single national stage. It took the name Vinitaly in the early 1970s and grew in lockstep with Italian wine's own transformation — from bulk-shipped table wine to Barolo, Brunello and Amarone commanding global respect. Verona was a natural home: the city sits at the crossroads of Valpolicella, Soave, Bardolino and Lugana, and its Veronafiere exhibition centre had hosted agricultural fairs since 1898.

Today it is the largest wine fair on earth devoted to a single country's production: roughly 4,000 exhibitors fill over 100,000 square metres of halls organised region by region, drawing around 90,000-plus attendees and buyers from well over 100 countries. Deals worth billions are struck over four days; ministers and prime ministers make ritual appearances; and since 2012 the invitation-only OperaWine gala (with Wine Spectator) has opened the week by pouring Italy's 100-odd most celebrated producers in a single room. The fair has weathered drama too — the 2020 edition was cancelled outright by the pandemic, returning with a special edition in late 2021 before roaring back to full scale.

As the main fair grew more strictly trade-only, Verona answered with Vinitaly and the City, launched in the mid-2010s as the 'fuori salone' for everyone else. For one long weekend the historic centre becomes a wine festival: tasting stands and wine talks fill Piazza dei Signori, the Loggia di Fra Giocondo, the Mercato Vecchio courtyard and, in recent editions, even the Arena di Verona. More than 70 events — guided tastings, masterclasses, music, food pairings — let the public drink what the buyers inside the fair are haggling over, against a backdrop of medieval towers and Roman stone.
whyGo: Nowhere else can you taste the entire Italian wine map — all 20 regions, thousands of producers — in one place, while the city outside throws a parallel street festival in some of northern Italy's loveliest squares. For a wine lover based in Zürich it may be the highest-value short trip of the year: five hours on a train and you're swirling Amarone a few kilometres from the vineyards it came from.

gettingThere.route: Zürich HB → Milano Centrale (EC direct, ~3h20) → Verona Porta Nuova (Frecciarossa/Frecciabianca, ~1h15)
gettingThere.notes: Book the Zürich–Milan EC as an SBB supersaver up to 60 days ahead and the Milan–Verona leg on Trenitalia/Italo separately — far cheaper than one through-ticket. Aim for the 07:33-ish EC to arrive in Verona by early afternoon.

gettingThere.route: Zürich (ZRH) → Milan Malpensa (~50 min), Malpensa Express to Milano Centrale, then train to Verona Porta Nuova; occasional seasonal ZRH→Verona (VRN) directs
gettingThere.notes: Flying saves little over the train once transfers are counted; only worth it if you catch a rare direct to Verona Villafranca airport (15 min bus to town).

gettingThere.route: Zürich → Gotthard tunnel → Milan → A4 east to Verona (~440 km)
gettingThere.cost: ~CHF 120–160 in fuel + Italian tolls (~€30 return); Swiss vignette required
gettingThere.notes: Gotthard queues are brutal on spring weekends and parking near Veronafiere/centro storico is scarce during the fair — the train is genuinely the better option here.

costs.notes: Verona hotel rates double or triple during Vinitaly week and sell out months out — accommodation is the whole game. Thrifty means advance-fare trains, a room in Vicenza/Peschiera/Desenzano (20–30 min by regional train) and the public Vinitaly and the City tastings (~€15–30 per tasting card). Midrange adds 2–3 nights in a Verona B&B plus a trade-fair day ticket (~€100–150). Splurge covers a centro storico hotel, OperaWine-adjacent dinners and Valpolicella cellar visits with a driver.

weather: Mid-April in Verona is proper spring: 8–19°C, generous sunshine broken by short showers, cool evenings for the outdoor piazza tastings. Pack layers, a light rain jacket, and comfortable shoes — the fairgrounds alone are a 100,000 m² hike.
tips: Know which event you're actually attending: Vinitaly proper (Sun–Wed) is a trade fair — you'll need trade/press accreditation or a pricey day ticket, and it's a working event with spittoons, not a festival. Casual visitors should target the Vinitaly and the City weekend instead.
Book accommodation 4–6 months out, or stay along the rail line (Vicenza, Peschiera del Garda, Desenzano) where prices stay sane and trains into Verona Porta Nuova run every 20–30 minutes.

If you do go inside the fair, plan by region and spit relentlessly — there are thousands of wines and the halls are organised region by region; pick three regions and work them properly rather than wandering.
For Vinitaly and the City, buy the tasting card early in the day, bring photo ID (18+ strictly enforced), and hit the Piazza dei Signori stands before the evening crush around 18:00–20:00.
Restaurants across the centro storico are fully booked with wine-trade dinners all week — reserve tables two to three weeks ahead or eat early.
Tack on a half-day in Valpolicella or Soave: cellars 20–30 minutes from Verona pour Amarone and Soave Classico at the source, and many run special open-cellar programmes during Vinitaly week.

localRecs.name: Antica Bottega del Vino
localRecs.note: Verona's temple of wine since 1890, steps from Piazza delle Erbe — a cellar of ~18,000 bottles and risotto all'Amarone; the after-hours living room of Vinitaly week.

localRecs.name: Osteria del Bugiardo
localRecs.note: Buzzing stand-up wine bar on Corso Porta Borsari pouring Buglioni's own Valpolicella by the glass with cicchetti — perfect between piazza tastings.

localRecs.name: Piazza delle Erbe & Torre dei Lamberti
localRecs.note: The old Roman forum turned market square; climb the 84 m tower for a view over the rooftops to the Vinitaly and the City action one square over.

localRecs.name: Arena di Verona
localRecs.note: The 1st-century Roman amphitheatre — better preserved than the Colosseum and, in recent editions, itself a Vinitaly and the City venue.

localRecs.name: Caffè Monte Baldo
localRecs.note: Old-school osteria near Piazza dei Signori doing polpette and crostini with honest local pours — a cheap, delicious counterweight to fair-week excess.

photos.caption: Inside a Veronafiere exhibition hall at Vinitaly, where thousands of producers pour region by region
Crowds moving between the Vinitaly pavilions at the Verona fairgrounds
Piazza dei Signori from above — the Renaissance heart of Verona and main stage of Vinitaly and the City
